text:
In the midst of a bustling forest, where golden rays filtered through canopy leaves, there lived a wise old woodcutter named Bartholomew. Bartholomew was seldom on foot, often using his sturdy axe to fell trees that blocked the path or served as sturdy shelters during nights spent outdoors. On one particularly chilly autumn day, his path led him to an ancient, gnarled oak tree that had stood unwavering for centuries.

Something about the tree unsettled him. Roots wriggling, branches bowing as though whispering secrets no soul could hear. The air around it felt charged, thick with quiet wisdom.

Driven by curiosity, not fear, Bartholomew reached out his hand. The touch was cool, solid. He pried away a burlap sack tightly sealed at the top. Inside lay an assortment of nuts—almonds, acorns, walnuts—an unopened box of pecans.

As he carefully unwrapped the box, a soft laughter
